query: evidence on radiotherapy causing radiation-induced osteosarcoma

retrieved_docs.content: Neurocognitive impairment Facial disfigurement (eg, facial bone hypoplasia) Endocrinopathies (eg, growth hormone deficiency, diabetes, central hypothyroidism, and ACTH deficiency) Ocular impairment (eg, cataracts, amblyopia, retinopathy, and decreased visual acuity) Nasal and oral complications (eg, odynophagia, chronic sinusitis, epistaxis, mucositis, and delayed tooth eruption) Dermatitis Intestinal obstruction Chronic otitis and hearing loss Urinary incontinence and frequency Vaginal stenosis Erectile dysfunction Joint stiffness and impaired mobility Skeletal growth impairment and pathological fractures Secondary malignancy [4] [32] [37] [36] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Complications -- Treatment-Related Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation adverse effects Radiation imparts superficial adverse effects, including skin dryness, itching, peeling, and, uncommonly, burns. Menstrual changes, erectile dysfunction, and infertility are all reported adverse events in cases of pelvic radiation. When the chest and abdomen are involved in radiation treatment, diarrhea, incontinence, rectal bleeding, nausea, vomiting, dry mouth, dysphagia, pneumonitis, and fibrosis are possible. Much like chemotherapy, a small risk of late development of a secondary malignancy is present. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoma -- Etiology -- Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Significantly contributes to a patient's long-term risk for developing STS The effects are dose-dependent, and they typically occur at the periphery of the radiation field Shorter disease-specific survival as compared to the spontaneous counterparts Children who develop STS post-radiation do so at a median of 11.8 years later and in a dose-dependent fashion

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Etiology -- Secondary Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary osteosarcoma is associated most commonly with Paget disease of bone and radiation exposure. [12] Osteosarcoma has been induced in animal models with beryllium, alkylating agents, the Finkel-Biskis-Jinkins (FBJ) virus, and the Rous sarcoma virus. [13] Case reports correlating an osteosarcoma diagnosis with a history of electrical burns, trauma, and joint arthroplasty have also been documented. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma cells are assumed to be relatively radioresistant, so radiotherapy is not routinely utilized to treat osteosarcoma. Radiotherapy may be used when margins are very close or positive after surgical resection, when surgery would be incredibly morbid, in the setting of metastatic disease or recurrent disease, and when a patient declines surgery. [51] [52] The recommended dose of radiation is 56 to 76 Gy, based on surgical margins and site. [36] [51] The NCCN specifically recommends 55 Gy if positive postoperative margins and 60 to 70 Gy for unresectable disease. ( NCCN Guidelines Bone Cancer )

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: All modalities of radiation administration have been utilized with no clear benefit of one over the other. Modern modalities such as stereotactic body radiotherapy and high linear energy transfer carbon-ion radiotherapy may increase the efficacy of radiotherapy for advanced or unresectable osteosarcoma. [51] A phase 3 international randomized controlled trial is underway investigating the efficacy of stereotactic body radiotherapy in patients with oligometastatic osteosarcoma (Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy in Patients with Rare Oligometastatic Cancers, OligoRARE, NCT04498767). Radiation toxicity includes mild and moderate dermatitis and late fibrosis. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma in adults is often secondary to another bone disorder. Approximately 1% of patients with Paget disease of bone develop osteosarcoma, although this risk may be decreasing as Paget disease is treated with bisphosphonates. [15] [16] Ionizing radiation is implicated in 3% of osteosarcoma cases, 4 to 40 years after exposure. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Radiation Oncology -- Secondary Malignancies
retrieved_docs.content: Either chemotherapy or radiotherapy may cause secondary malignancies. Radiotherapy can induce osteosarcomas, although many other solid tumors have been reported. A recent systemic review of patients with Ewing sarcoma reported the cumulative incidence of secondary malignancies at 20.5% at 30 years, with 63% being solid tumors. [43] Retrospective analysis suggests that the cumulative risk of second malignancies is dose-dependent, with 0 cases at doses <48 Gy and 130 reported cases at doses exceeding 60 Gy. [44]

retrieved_docs.content: Samarium 153-EDTMP Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS)

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Radiation Oncology -- Radiation Timing
retrieved_docs.content: RT is typically given either preoperatively within 24 hours or postoperatively within 72 hours. If surgery is planned, it is ideal that the patient be consulted and consented to before surgery since the anesthesia may render the patient unable to give informed consent. Also, the patient could be in more pain postoperatively and may not be able to lie still during treatment. However, in trauma or emergencies, the radiation would be given postoperatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- Complications -- Treatment-Related Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary malignancy A combination of the chemotherapy administered, diagnostic irradiation, and radiotherapy, in addition to possible underlying genetic cancer predispositions, all likely contribute to the risk of a secondary malignancy. Secondary leukemias usually present in the first 10 years after osteosarcoma treatment, while secondary solid cancers are observed at any point. [81]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Research has identified associations with secondary osteosarcoma in patients with Paget disease, electrical burns, trauma, exposure to beryllium, exposure to alkylating agents, FBJ virus, osteochondromatosis, enchondromatosis, fibrous dysplasia, orthopedic prosthetics as well as bone infarction and infection. Additionally, osteosarcoma reportedly correlates with exposure to ionizing radiation, radium, and archaic contrast agents such as thorotrast. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Heterotopic Ossification Prophylaxis -- Radiation Oncology -- Toxicity from Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary malignancies from radiation can develop in the bone or tissues included in a previous RT field. Since the total dose given for HO prophylaxis is very low, the changes of secondary malignancies are also extremely low, but not zero. At least 2 case reports have described secondary malignancies after a single fraction. The first case described a 51-year-old patient who received 7 Gy x 1 for HO 15 years earlier. He underwent a second course of 7 Gy x 1 for debilitating HO but developed high-grade undifferentiated sarcoma of the proximal thigh 16 months after his second treatment. [26] Another case described a 26-year-old male who underwent surgery for an acetabular fraction and received 7 Gy in 1 fraction postoperatively for HO prophylaxis. He presented 11 years later with osteosarcoma of the treated area, which was treated definitively with neoadjuvant cisplatin and adriamycin, with plans for surgical resection at the time of the report. [27] Otherwise, there are no other reported cases of secondary malignancies from RT given for HO prophylaxis treatment, as it is a rare complication.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma(Archived) -- Radiation Oncology -- Post-Operative Radiation Treatment for Primary Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Resectable Tumors- Post-operative radiation therapy with 55 Gy plus 9 to 13 Gy boost for residual microscopic or gross disease (64-68 total dose) Unresectable Tumors- 60 to 70 Gy (the total dose is dependent on tolerance on normal tissue)

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Radiation Oncology -- Growth Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: The growth plates are moderately sensitive to radiation. Doses of 10 Gy to the epiphyseal plate can result in stunted growth, and a whole of >20 Gy can lead to complete arrest—which can lead to significant length discrepancies between the irradiated and unirradiated limbs. Younger patients (<15 years old) tend to have limb shortening, while those over 15 do not appear to be at risk, perhaps due to growth plate closure. [46] Limb atrophy may be found in up to 80% of patients. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Radiation Oncology -- Late Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: The late toxicities, which can occur months to years after treatment, are usually the most problematic for long-term survivors compared to the acute side effects. Like acute side effects, they largely depend on the irradiated location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is considered a subdiscipline of radiation therapy, indicating that it falls under the broader field of radiation therapy, which encompasses the clinical use of ionizing radiation for treatment purposes, including both external beam radiotherapy and nuclear medicine techniques. This hierarchical relationship highlights that therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine are integral components of radiation therapy, contributing to the management of cancer and other conditions through targeted radiation-based interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: Intrakavitární radioterapie is a specialized form of radiation therapy that targets lesions within body cavities; the two terms are closely related and may be considered synonymous in clinical practice, reflecting the same therapeutic approach of delivering ionizing radiation to treat intracavitary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 27
retrieved_docs.content: radiation surgery (الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية). Overall, the hierarchy connects general radioactive‑isotope examinations to specific imaging and therapeutic techniques: transmission imaging and myocardial imaging derive from the broad radioactive‑isotope examination; nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures branch into radionuclide localization of tumor and radioisotope studies of the central nervous system; radionuclide imaging actions serve as methods for these procedures; and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine link diagnostic imaging to radiation‑based treatments such as radiation therapy, intracavitary radiotherapy, and stereotactic radiation surgery. This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: The “Allowed qualifier” designation indicates that radiotherapy is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for certain medical conditions but not for others. Radiotherapy is an approved treatment modality for ورم الشفة (lip tumor), where it can be used alone or in combination with surgery to achieve local control of malignant disease. In the context of متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة (AIDS), radiotherapy is not a direct therapy for the immunodeficiency itself; however, it may be employed as an allowed intervention for opportunistic malignancies that can arise in patients with AIDS, such as Kaposi sarcoma or non‑Hodgkin lymphoma. Conversely, radiotherapy is not a standard or appropriate intervention for nervous system congenital anomalies; structural developmental defects of the nervous system are generally managed by surgical, rehabilitative, or supportive measures rather than by radiation. Recognizing these distinctions helps clinicians apply radiotherapy where it is evidence‑based—such as for lip tumors and certain cancers in AIDS—while avoiding its use in conditions like congenital nervous system anomalies where it offers no therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 5 -- Community 2919
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier “ošetřování” (treatment) is applicable to the medical conditions ورم (tumor), عضال غدي (glandular tumor), and كسر عنق عظم الفخذ (fracture of the femoral neck), indicating that therapeutic care is appropriate for each of these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as dاء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). Dاء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest, and it is also clinically associated with قساح. Radiotherapy is an allowed qualifier for both dاء الكريات المنجلية and respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult), and the use of radiotherapy can be qualified by قساح. These connections indicate that radiotherapy may be considered in the therapeutic context of sickle cell disease and adult respiratory failure, and that the presence of قساح influences how radiotherapy is qualified or applied.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1979
retrieved_docs.content: Other nuclear medicine imaging techniques rely on radionuklidy, which serves as the radioactive substance used in these procedures to visualize physiological processes. The incorporation of radionuklidy is central to the diagnostic capability of such imaging, allowing clinicians to assess organ function, detect disease, and guide therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 801
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging, Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, and الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) are all subdomains of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes). As child specialties of this overarching nuclear‑medicine approach, Transmission imaging focuses on acquiring diagnostic images through the detection of emitted radiation, Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine applies radioactive substances for treatment of disease, and الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية utilizes precisely targeted radiation beams to perform minimally invasive surgical interventions. Recognizing these fields as components of the broader radioactive‑isotope examination framework highlights their interconnected roles in diagnosis, therapy, and advanced procedural techniques within nuclear medicine.
